title: Caprifolium caeruleum
taxonomy: Caprifolium caeruleum is a species of flowering plant in the family Caprifoliaceae, commonly known as the honeysuckle family. It belongs to the genus Caprifolium, which is closely related to the genus Lonicera. The plant is native to Europe and Western Asia, and its classification is as follows: kingdom - Plantae, clade - Angiosperms, clade - Eudicots, clade - Asterids, order - Dipsacales, family - Caprifoliaceae, genus - Caprifolium, species - C. caeruleum. The classification of Caprifolium caeruleum reflects its evolutionary relationships with other flowering plants. The plant's classification is also a key factor in understanding its characteristics and behavior.

preparations: Caprifolium caeruleum, also known as the blue honeysuckle or woodbine, has been used in herbal preparations for its medicinal properties. The plant's flowers, leaves, and stems are used to create teas, infusions, and tinctures that are believed to have antiseptic and anti-inflammatory effects. intro: Caprifolium caeruleum, also known as Blue Honeysuckle, is a deciduous shrub native to China, belonging to the Caprifoliaceae family. The plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine for its numerous health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antiviral properties, which have been shown to aid in treating respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and coughs. The bioactive constituents of Caprifolium caeruleum include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made from this plant include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to treat respiratory problems and support immune system function.
